speaker: interviewer
text: This is June 21, 1995. We're talking with Mr. Harry Toporek. This is Lincolnwood, Illinois, USA. Tape number six. Mr. Toporek, could you please let us know what you've been doing professionally since you've been in this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came here, I start working in a tannery on Webster Avenue here in Chicago. And I was very happy in the beginning. But I have seen there is no future for me there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided that I'm going to become a tradesman. Because the education, what they need in here to become a professor, or lawyer, or doctor-- I haven't got it. So whatever I'm going to do, I will have to work hard. So, if I have to work hard, let me make the most that I can.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I decided to become actually anything in the trade-- but I tried to be electrician. That wasn't successful, because the union-- I couldn't get in in the union. So the easiest thing for me is to become a carpenter. And I became a carpenter. And I worked for about a year, year and a half. And I built a lot. I borrowed money-- bought a lot, and I start building a building-- single resident. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have seen there is a big opportunity in the building industry, and I-- and I make the best use out of it. I was at the right time, the right time, right time, and right place, the right profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been happy in that field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you been happy in that field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, very. Very.

speaker: interviewer
text: What have you done in terms of other projects?

speaker: interviewee
text: I build-- at the beginning, I built single residents. Then later on I start building apartment buildings. And I build a lot of them. And big ones, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So actually, in 1970, when my wife passed away, I had 15 apartment buildings-- big buildings-- with all my empire, I became a poor man. You say that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: She passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: She passed away in 1970. And what was happening in your life at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened in her life?

speaker: interviewer
text: In your life at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was-- that was worse than going through a concentration camp. At that time, I was-- to the concentration camp I was accosted. That punishment, I couldn't take. I was-- that was final for me. I thought that I will never survive that pain. But somehow, I had children. I had to go on with living. But I stop building. I didn't build another building after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any special way that you sought to commemorate your-- to honor your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I worked with my wife. When my wife was still alive, we decided that we will make some kind of a-- memorialize the 6 million. And the best thing would be is a synagogue in a public place. But I didn't want to do it on my own. And to-- to memorize also my parents, as part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we decided that we'd find, in Tucson, Arizona, that somebody was making museum-- mosaic. And we contacted the individual that was there. I was there with my wife. And we decided that we're going to go make this thing from mosaic.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were going to put something like this. And he showed us-- as a matter of fact, I looked downstairs. I got these pictures of synagogues and churches the way he designed it. But in the meantime, my wife got sick, and she died. She passed away.

speaker: interviewer
text: But this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I decided that I'm going to go on my own and finish up the project. And I decided that I'm going to memorialize the 6 million, including my wife, and make a mural, what is in the congregation Ezras Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that located, that synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Lunt and California. It's 7001 North California Avenue.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the city of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Chicago. I belong to that church, synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we appreciate you sharing-- sharing everything with us. It's always very hard to include everything in an interview. It has been 50 years. Is there anything else that you would care to add, any-- any last remark that you would like to share with the people who will be viewing this all around the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing I could say-- that they should not make the mistake what we made. If people will say there are certain places that they kill Jews, believe them. We did not believe. I don't know how much we could have done if we would believe. But we should have believed, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] because I mentioned this thing in the tape, that people came in 1941, and they told us that they killed the people. We did not want to believe. The difference between is only to believe. And not just in-- in-- in the religious point of view. The difference between-- we didn't want to believe that that's going to happen to us. The Jews in Israel, they did believe that they want. They want to throw them into the sea, they did believe. This is what they want. And this is what they're going to do. And no more can something like this happen again. I mean, you've got nothing to lose. You got everything to gain. That's proven with Israel, after four wars. And they could not succeed. We are determined to live, [NON-ENGLISH]. This is a nation that cannot be-- indestructible nation. When I was-- three years ago, when I was in Poland, I have seen things in the synagogues. The way they-- you know, I was in [NON-ENGLISH], I looked at those things. Now you look today with different eyes. The world didn't say anything. Nobody cared what's happened to us. The whole world was aware of it. And I'm not talking about the average people-- people what were-- that could help us, but they didn't help us. If you're not going to help ourselves, nobody will take care of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I'd like to thank you for helping, because that's you're doing today.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what you're doing today.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry, I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're very glad--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was expecting it to go smoother.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, it's not a smooth issue. It's not a smooth issue. And again, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it's a funny thing that I said Friday. It's catching up, and it does.

speaker: interviewer
text: It does. It really does. Thank you very much, Mr. Toporek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Toporek, when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was taken, I would say, I'm here about 10,11 years old. It's me about 60 years ago. This is my entire family. My father, mother, brothers, and sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the brothers' and sisters' names? Could you go through them--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And give us their first names?

speaker: interviewee
text: From right to left, my oldest brother is Efroim. Next to Efroim was my brother, Joe. Then my sister, Frieda. Next to Frieda is my brother, Lazar. Next to Lazar is my brother, Leip. The last to the left is my brother, Sam. He lives here in Chicago. Then going back from left to right, my sister Bluma, my mother, and my father to the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken when my brother, Joe, went to the army. I would say about 10 years before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what location?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lask.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you please tell us who the people are in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the right is the rabbi for the city of Lask, Rabbi Isenberg. Rabbi Isenberg. Leibele Isenberg, we use to call him. To the left is Moshe [NON-ENGLISH], this is his name. He was an associate of the rabbi. I mean, this was taken 1937 in Lask, by Roman Washing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if it's just what we're seeing on the video-- Could you please tell us who the people are in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is my uncle and aunt with his entire family. His name is uncle Joseph. This is my father's brother what live in the city. None of those people survive. Not a one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us their first names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I know them all. Sure. The first lady from the left, her name was Shetl. Then there's Moshe. Then was Leah-- the girl in the middle. Then come [NON-ENGLISH]. Then was Lazar, to the right. Now the front from right to left. This is Rivka. This is Joseph-- this is the uncle. And this is the baby there. Simon is the name, Simon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taking approximately 1937, '38.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lask, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell us about this picture. Who as this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's me when I was 15, 15 and 1/2-- 15 and 1/2 years old. This was taken also in Lask, Poland. I remember like yesterday when I took those pictures home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Toporek, where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken on May the 9th, 1945, in the place I was liberated. Unfortunate, those people were burned alive. They were pushed in-- maybe before, a couple hours before, they were put in a barn and burned to death-- all of them were gone. I don't think besides me and somebody else, another friend, that other people survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, what area was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between Schwerin and Parchim in Germany. Where this location was, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again in the similar photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken also May the 9th by the same Russian officer, what actually came the next morning to liberate us, to liberate me and my-- and most of those people were also burned alive in the barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in this photo, who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my late wife, Faye and myself. This picture was taken in Lodz in September, 1945. I was 22 and 1/2 years. So was my late wife, 22 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is an unusual photograph. What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I moved into 5740 Kedzie Avenue in Chicago, I did want to make memorialized, put a star on the-- on the cement. Like the star had six-- six parts of it. The Star of David. And to put in each star, I would like to put in-- that time I like to put in the concentration camp. But my wife disapproved on it. She said, I don't want to raise children on a cemetery. So the whole thing collapsed. I mean I didn't put anything. At least I put the star as is. And this is still remaining here on June the 22nd-- or the 21st-- 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why don't you go ahead and describe. Go ahead, Marsha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this photo? What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, this is a painting that I dedicated to the synagogue in 1972, on May 2nd. This is a-- I made a memorial for the 6 million, and dedicated in my wife's memory, because she also was part of the story. And she also died in 1970-- so I put the Star of David. And you could see the top for the Star of David is my late wife, Faye Toporek. To my left-- to the left of the angle of the thing is my mother, my mother, Esther. To the right is my father, Moshe. To the left bottom is Anne Frank. She's writing the way she writes her memoirs. And to the right, that is [NON-ENGLISH], the way he is going with the children to their doom in Majdanek from Warsaw. To bottom from the Star of David, this is a mountain of shoes. The artist-- the shoes for so many of them that they went out of line. And to the left, there are the crematoriums in Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Now, this thing is part of Israel. This painting was dedicated-- was made in two parts. One is part of the survivors, the Holocaust. The second is from Israel, that Israel has come in existence. And this is an old man. He's telling the children of Israel what's happened during the years from 1939 until 1945 to the Jewish people. And you've got people in Israel from all nationalities. But they're all Jewish. They're Jewish people. And he tells them the story. To the far right, this is the Warsaw ghetto uprising. They could see Mordechai Anielewicz in midst of the flame, fighting for his life in the Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, the artist's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The artist's name was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And where's the original of this located?

speaker: interviewee
text: The original is located in the synagogue congregation Ezras Israel, on 7001 North California Avenue, Lunt and California in city of Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby is my granddaughter, Pamela. She's kissing the great-grandma. This is Barbara's mother, Mrs. Meckel. She was here when she was about two years old. She kisses the grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken right here in this house, in this place, in this living room. From the grandmother mother to the grandchild. Grandmother to grandchild.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Toporek, who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest grandchild, Pamela. This picture is taken, she's 11 years old, in Washington, DC.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my other granddaughter, the youngest of my Esther, daughter Esther. Her name is Jennifer. She's seven years old. This picture was taking out in Washington and sent to me here in Chicago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are also our grandchildren. The girl is Laura. She is the son-- Barbara son's grandchildren. And the little one is John-John. This was taken about a year ago. He mailed to us. They live in New York state. And it was send to us to have their pictures here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our grandchild from Robert Glickman-- this is Rachel. She's two years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taking in Portland, Oregon and mailed to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother, Sam with my sister-in-law, Renee. With him, I was all the years in concentration camp. And they're both living here in Chicago-- I mean the Chicago area. He lives in Skokie, I live in Lincolnwood.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was taken in 1945 or so. And they just got married in Germany. And it's about 50 years ago. Let's put it this way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Toporek, can you please introduce us to this lovely group standing behind you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish I could see who was behind me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Just turn and--

speaker: interviewee
text: If I would have the-- I shouldn't touch that? Oh. To my right, this is Jennifer, my granddaughter. Say hello. To my left, I can see Jack on top above me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Barbara, my wife. Then there's Esther, my daughter, and Pamela, my granddaughter. Isn't it a lovely family? It certainly is. You're very lucky. This is very important, what you did today. We're delighted that you shared this with us. Would anyone care to add to those feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks, Dad. I know it wasn't easy. We all appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely.

speaker: interviewee
text: You got something to say? No, that was nice. I'm glad that I'm here, that I can tell the story because in another 15 to 20 years from now, it won't be anybody. They try to deny it, so long as we're here-- at least it's going to be proved, and from hundreds of people, from thousands of people that whatever happened there, the Holocaust, that this was not a premeditated thing. It was not generated by the Jews. Only operated by the Germans, by the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the end of it I can say. Thank you.
